findings.title: Simulation mode can no longer dwell-click (or edge-scroll) inside the embedded browser â€” stationary mouse stops the page dwell clock
findings.file: D:/latest_gaze_connect/latest_v4/latest_v4/gazeconnect_v4/src/screens/WebBrowsingScreen.tsx (lines ~3764-3871, commit 52cb308) interacting with electron/browser/browserGazeController.ts lines 1608-1614 (commit d9fb816)
findings.scenario: Run `.\start-dev.bat --simulate` (or hit start-dev.bat's documented silent hardware->simulation fallback), open YouTube/Quick Search, move the mouse onto a video card and hold it still â€” the natural dwell gesture. The in-page cursor ring sits on the card and NEVER fills; no click ever fires, no matter how long the user waits. A later mouse twitch doesn't help: the gap-pause shifts state.start forward by the whole gap, so progress stays ~0. Completing a click now requires continuously wiggling the mouse with <150ms between events, inside the 48px stability radius, for the full dwellMs (850-2800ms). Edge-scroll in armed watch mode dies the same way (scroll bursts run per forwarded frame in main.ts's webview:updateGaze handler). Before this range, parking the mouse clicked normally. This violates the stated invariant that simulation mode 'must still drive the in-page browser cursor', and docs Entry 9's claim 'a mousemove path keeps simulation mode working' is only true for cursor MOTION, not selection.
findings.evidence: The injected dwell timer ticks only inside window.gcUpdateAndPoll, whose sole caller is buildGazeUpdateAndPollScript executed from the `ipcMain.handle('webview:updateGaze', ...)` handler (electron/main.ts:1854,1931) â€” there is no page-side rAF/interval ticking dwell. The renderer's only senders are in the forward() effect: `const unsub = ws.subscribeGaze(() => forward());` (no WS frames exist in simulation) and `const onMouse = () => { if (!hasRealGazeRef.current) forward(); }` on 'mousemove' â€” a physically stationary mouse fires zero mousemove events, so zero ticks. The replaced code (52cb308~1) was `const interval = setInterval(() => { ... if (dist < 1.5) { browser.updateGazeCursor(prev.x, prev.y, ...); return; } ... }, 33)` â€” it re-sent the held position every 33ms, which is what made park-to-click work. The accidental fallback (one twitch after dwellMs computing elapsed=now-start>dwellMs and instantly clicking) is neutralized by d9fb816: `if (cfgGap.gapPauseEnabled !== false && lastFrameTs > 0 && dtMs > Number(cfgGap.gapPauseMs || 150)) { if (state.start > 0) state.start += dtMs; ... }` â€” every >150ms event gap contributes zero progress. Fix direction: when !hasRealGaze, keep a low-rate keepalive (e.g. 33ms re-send of gpRef while browser.isOpen) or exempt the simulation path from gapPause.

findings.title: TTS rate/volume/Hindi-language settings are silently dead whenever the backend is connected (the normal state)
findings.scenario: Patient/caregiver sets TTS volume low (night-time), slows the speech rate, or sets ttsLanguage='hindi' in Settings. With the backend connected â€” which is always, in normal operation â€” every SPEAK now goes only through ws.speak(text): full volume 1.0, fixed rate 150, default English SAPI5 voice. Hindi phrases (a core app constraint: 'Bilingual: English + Hindi') are mangled by an English voice or skipped; mute/quiet/slow settings do nothing. Before this commit the settings-honoring browser voice was always audible (overlapped by the default backend voice â€” the double-speak being fixed), so at minimum one correct-language, correct-rate, correct-volume voice played. The fix removed the only code path that ever honored these settings.
findings.evidence: New code: `if (ws.isConnected && ws.speak) { ws.speak(text); } else { speakText(text, ttsRate, ttsVolume, ttsLanguage || 'english'); }`. speakText (App.tsx:143-159) is the only place ttsRate/ttsVolume/ttsLanguage are applied (`u.lang = 'hi-IN'` for Hindi, `if (volume <= 0) return` for mute). ws.speak sends text only: `speak: (text) => send('speak', { text })` (useWebSocket.tsx:608). The API methods that could sync settings to the backend â€” `setTTSRate`/`setTTSVolume` (useWebSocket.tsx:610-611) â€” are never called anywhere in src (grep confirms only their definition), and the backend handler `'speak': lambda: self._speak(data.get('text', ''))` (python/main.py:1860) takes no rate/volume/language; the engine is hardcoded to rate=150, volume=1.0, first 'female' voice (python/main.py:689-700). Minimal fix: call ws.setTTSRate/ws.setTTSVolume when settings change and pass language (or pick voice by Devanagari detection) in the backend speak path.

findings.title: Browser dwell-progress resume keys on the sticky-ghost targetKey, so a click can commit with gaze never re-entering the target â€” and faster than before
findings.scenario: Patient dwells 60-80% on a YouTube card, then shifts gaze to the title text 50-80px below the thumbnail to read it (within the 80px STICKY_TOLERANCE_BROWSER). Stability breaks for one frame (save captured), but the sticky ghost keeps synthesizing clickReq with the same targetKey while gaze is parked outside the rect; the next still frame has distâ‰ˆ0 so stabilityHeld is true, the resume fires (progress jumps back to 60-80%), Phase A anchors the cursor onto the card center, and the click commits ~200-500ms later â€” the video opens while the patient is reading, having never looked back at the thumbnail. Pre-existing sticky behavior could also misfire this way, but only after a FULL fresh onset+dwell (~1.1-3.1s) of parked off-target gaze; the retention commit cuts that warning window by the saved fraction. The app cursor does not share this shortcut: its save path nulls dwellTargetRef/onsetTargetRef (GazeCursor.tsx:858-862), so its sticky path has no surviving target and resume requires the hit test/nearest-center to genuinely re-acquire the element.
findings.evidence: Resume condition is key-equality only: `if (state.savedProgressKey && state.savedProgressKey === state.targetKey && !state.clicked && ...) { state.start = now - resumeElapsed; }` (1380-1384). state.targetKey survives the break via the ghost: `if (!clickReq && state.targetKey && state.targetRect) { ... clickReq = { kind: 'sticky_resume', key: state.targetKey, rect: sRect } }` (1242-1259) runs BEFORE the break branch, so the break branch's `state.targetKey = newTargetKey` (1340) re-assigns the same key, and the different-target invalidation `if (newTargetKey && state.savedProgressKey && newTargetKey !== state.savedProgressKey)` (1345) never fires. stabilityHeld is gaze-stillness, not on-targetness: `const stabilityHeld = dist < stabilityRadius || (sameTarget && insideTargetRegion)` (1288) â€” dist is sample-to-sample distance, ~0 for parked gaze anywhere. A guard such as requiring gaze within the target rect (+ slack) at resume time, or excluding kind==='sticky_resume' frames from triggering resume, would restore the app-cursor semantics the commit message claims to mirror.
